speaker: interviewer
text: When are you-- when you say go--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll tell you when. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is 3/10/95. The survivor we are speaking with is Regina Weber. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: The interviewer is Raquel Grunwald. We're in a city of Great Neck, Long Island. The country is US-- USA. And the language we will be using is English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you have to shut the radio--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hello, my name is Raquel Grunwald, G-R-U-N-W-A-L-D. And I am conducting an interview with Regina Weber. And we're in Great Neck, Long Island, New York, USA. The date is March--

speaker: interviewer
text: --10th, '95. 1995. Can you tell me your name?

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell your first name and your last name for us?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you called Regina, or did you have another name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the times, in Hungarian, they called Re-gee-na, or Re-geh-na,

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names, like any Jewish names or Hebrew names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father and my mother always called me Rifkele.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How do you spell Rifkele?

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's sort of like a term of endearment?

speaker: interviewee
text: A term of endearment. That's the way I was called all the--

speaker: interviewer
text: That-- then that's also like a nickname.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's one's Jewish or Hebrew name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And can you give us your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this May I will be 68 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me the city that you were born in and the country. And you'll need to spell them both for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I was born in Kisvarda, Hungary, and I can spell it. Kisvarda, K-I-S-V-A-R-D-A. Hungary, H-U-N-G-A-R-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And Kisvarda was a small village or a big town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a town. I think we had 20,000 people living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many Jews would you think would have been in that town?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Give me your mother's maiden name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do they call her Terez? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they used to call her Pap Terez, because she was the daughter of a chief rabbi, and pap means rabbi, chief rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- well, she was known. That's Pap Terez, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your father's name? Can you give us his--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Hungarian name is Menyhert, which is a little difficult, but I'll spell--

speaker: interviewee
text: But they also used to call him in his Jewish name, which is Mendel, M-E-N-D-E-L.

speaker: interviewee
text: Menyhert-- do you want me to spell it?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and his last name was Katz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have any idea of his birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I now know it. He was born in 1890.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And do you know the date of his death?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I found out. It was 1960. 19-- Oh, 1960.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, he-- he survived the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us how that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father has been in America in the '30s. He was a big wine wholesaler. And he was going to sell some wine, even to Macy's. But then he didn't like America. He said, I don't want to bring up my children here. It's like a gypsy market. And he came back in about two weeks. And he had a businessman's visa. And when it came to be 1938, '39, my mother begged him. In fact, she used a very strong word. She said, [SPEAKING HUNGARIAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: That means save, but very strongly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father refused up until the end of 1939, when it was very difficult to get out of the country. What he did-- and he arrived here January, in this country, about 19-- 1940. And there was no way of bringing us out here. Because Hungary-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --went to war with America--

speaker: interviewer
text: So and your father was in America?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was he attempting to do in 1940, when he was here?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was still hoping-- he was hoping and going to the government and working on it, but it was too late.

speaker: interviewee
text: On our-- getting us out. But he left. He didn't listen to my mother. Because I guess he felt that his children will get away from religion. I think that was if we come to this country. And he just postponed it, and it was too late, unfortunately, when he came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel that your father was afraid to bring the family to America, because they would become not-Orthodox Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that is true. Because my brother started to get away from it, you know. And my household was a very intellectual because of my mother being so intelligent. And I think that what he was afraid of.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm going to take you back a little bit now. If you can give me the names of the children-- first of all, how many children were there in your family?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now give me their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest one was called Yolan. And we called her [? Cipi, ?] which was an endearing name, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Second one, [? Chicha, ?] or Berta. But we called her [? Chicha, ?] and we still do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Third one, Izabella. And the fourth, Phillip.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his Jewish name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or Rifkele. And my little sister, Hannah, Helen, [? Hannaleh, ?] you know, we were all--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How old was Yolan around-- I don't need exact dates at all-- [SIGHS] --when-- when-- when-- March 19, 1944, when the Nazis-- the Nilosh-- the Nazis marched into Budapest, how old was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's very difficult for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: She couldn't have been more than 24--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it just so happens that almost all the children were born two years apart.

speaker: interviewee
text: Phillip or [? Phishel ?] must have been about 19.

speaker: interviewer
text: A bright teenager. And Regina, you were around?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And the baby sister, Helen?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was five years younger than I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now tell us, please, of-- of these-- this whole group, including your mother, who was not alive after the war was over in 1945? And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and youngest little sister were gassed the night we arrived in Auschwitz, which was May 31, 1944. My older sister, Yolan, or [? Cipi, ?] was dragged to Belsen-- Bergen-Belsen. And we heard, from other people, that she died of typhoid when the war ended already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was alive until the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until the very bitter end, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's quite ironic. And so everybody else-- there was-- you--

speaker: interviewee
text: There are four of us alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and-- and you, can you tell us where you all live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Well, I live in Great Neck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Izabel lives in Manhattan, on the west side. My sister, [? Chicha, ?] lives in Binghamton, New York. And my brother, Phillip, lives in Montreal, Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell us about what happened with Nyirtass and what of-- kind of town it was? How it-- how-- how it was for the Jewish people in Nyirtass?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were a lot of poor people, poor Jews. And they lived on this Jewish street called Csillag Utca.

speaker: interviewer
text: It didn't mean Jewish star, just star.

speaker: interviewee
text: --street. And there were a number of-- there was even a banker. There were a number of Jewish people who did very well. And my father and mother were very well thought of because my mother was an exceptionally intelligent woman. And my father was terribly bright. So we were a family with what you would consider [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] , You want to translate that for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's very difficult. More like upper class, or you know, we just--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think we lived a nice life. And I just loved Kisvarda. I was a little girl, and I had loads of friends. And I went to a very good, private school. And I thought, there's no place like Kisvarda--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and I never want to leave it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whereas, my older sisters, as they got older, they all wanted to go to Budapest. And everybody in the world, but I-- just give me Kisvarda. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very well known in my little town.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you think there were around 3,000 Jews in that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I think that they brought them all in the Kisvarda ghetto. So I think that maybe Kisvarda and vicinity--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- you said your father was a wine merchant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was his main occupation. He would buy up vineyards before they even picked the grapes. It was a big business.

speaker: interviewer
text: We had-- under our house we had a giant basement with giant vats of wine, you know? And the-- the smaller dealers would come from the country and buy wine from my father. From the country? You mean even towns that were smaller?

speaker: interviewee
text: The towns were very much smaller.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they all had a bar.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was for the Hungarians. And later on, my father always ex-- exported to Switzerland, I think Holland, and maybe Denmark, wine. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family was fairly well-to-do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not all the time. It depends. It's a big business. If the wine came in successfully--

speaker: interviewee
text: --yes. And if not, well, we had a little economic problem, like every business has its ups and downs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember a Shabbat in your house in Kisvarda?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us a little?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were a large family. And my mother always would have two poor little boys who went to school in my town. So there were eight of us, and two more people. That's 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: The two little boys would come from where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Come for-- other towns, studying in Kisvarda. And they needed to go somewhere on the Shabbat. And my mother seeked them out. And so we always had at least 10 people at the table.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a beautiful dining room with this giant table and a white cloth, the damask cloth. Or sometimes, we even had a tablecloth that my mother designed. She was very, very talented. And with her, all futuristic designs--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had 32 candles burning every Shabbat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbat candles. Because we came from a very big family from my mother's side, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that all those who died. I think that was it. But it was beautiful, all those lights, candelabras, and then smaller ones. It was a beautiful occasion. Every Shabbat was beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the table was set beautifully?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother would bake her own challah?

speaker: interviewee
text: And always a couple of maids. And I used to help in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always loved the kitchen. And I loved to be around my mother, and the maids. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Even at 17, you loved it? Simply--

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know? And yes, there was fresh, homemade challah every single Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there music? Did they sing--

speaker: interviewee
text: --yes. Yes, my father and my brother. The girls didn't take such role in this whole Judaism. You know? We were kind of in the background.

speaker: interviewer
text: So none of the girls went to Jewish schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but they knew how to read the prayer books. I, when it came to me, I didn't even know how to read the prayer books.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they learn how to read the prayer books?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because in the school there would be one--

speaker: interviewee
text: Public school once a week. There would be a Jewish teacher coming and teaching the children read Hebrew, and some history, I'm sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you mean the Jewish children were taken out to be taught Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: They weren't taken out. The Jewish teacher came to the school. When the Catholic children went to their priest--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, or the priest came, where the Jewish children had not a rabbi, but a teacher--

speaker: interviewer
text: So each religion had a place in the public schools as well?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how come you didn't learn how to read Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wasn't very much interested. You know? I was a very good student, very busy with my homework.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I just got away with it. Let's put it that way. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't, somehow, in my interest. What were you interested in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a very, very funny child. I-- I used to-- anytime guests came, I had to entertain the guests. They put me on top of the table, and I had to entertain. I could sing. I could dance. And I could recite poetry. And little by little, the whole city, Kisvarda, knew about me as somebody who's always appearing in all the school plays. I once had five roles in the school plays. It was so much that they changed my name for the fifth one-- [LAUGHTER] --because it was, you know, Regina Katz was in everything. And I used to wear this Hungarian uniform for these occasions, which is very beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh well, it's like a peasant dress, white with a lot of ribbons on it, and a little vest from velvet. And-- [LAUGHS] --I looked very cute.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] So you had a-- you had a real fun life?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your dreams-- of-- what were your dreams? Of-- of becoming what? As you were getting into--

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess everybody thought that I'm just going to be a little actress. You know? I mean, this was nothing I could do about, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that was in the beginning, in the-- in-- while we were in Hungary, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kisvarda. No, I did go once to Budapest, and recited a poem there. And it-- it looked like I may become a professional.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the schools you attended--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and how-- how long you attended them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, at the age-- oh, I went to nursery school. And I even remember a little coat I had, a blue coat with white fur around it. And I once got lost in nursery school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the police found me. And they brought me home, put me on the table. And everybody was kissing me. You know? Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: But after nursery school, I-- at age six or close to it, I got into-- just elementary school.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my oldest sisters preceded me in the same school. And we were known as the Katz sisters. And they all gotta be A's, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no question about it. You know? The school was waiting for us in a way, you know? So I had a standing in the school, you know? So it felt good going to school every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I loved going to school. Yes, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever experience antisemitism as a child in the schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so much in the schools. But once you got out of the school, there were these hooligans, young boys, who would call you dirty Jew or all kinds of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is before the Germans came in. Because the Hungarians themselves have a history of being antisemitic, maybe for 1,000 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you walked down the streets, it was a little bit uncomfortable for you?

speaker: interviewer
text: But in the school itself, it was of comfort?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I had some comfort because I was a good student, and I had a station, let's say, in life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. How about your brother? Did he go to public school too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father didn't let him go to public school. Didn't let-- didn't like the Jewish school, my father, for some reason. He was a individual, and he believed in what he believed in. And my oldest-- my-- one of my sisters, [? Chicha-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: --has taught her at home-- taught him at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you mean your father didn't want him to go to a Jewish yeshiva or a cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, then when he was old-- yes, he went to cheder, reluctantly. He never wanted to go. And he went to yeshiva. And occasionally, he was on top, the best student. And then he would leave it and become more modern. You know, take--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of-- what kind of cheder and yeshiva was this? Was it Hasidic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't call it Hasidic, because it was like one grade away from it. But I don't know what they would call it, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the children have payots, like side locks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, but he pushed it up. My father pushed it up too. And my father didn't wear a shtreimel. He wore a beaver hat.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now tell us what a shtreimel is, and tell us what a beaver hat is.

speaker: interviewee
text: A shtreimel is the most Orthodox, which is very similar to what the Russian Jews wore, whom we never knew. It's a velvet cap with, I think-- I think fur, which was a very good fur.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mink all around it, but my father didn't believe in that.

speaker: interviewee
text: A beaver hat is very much like a very fine-- and hat made out of, I guess, beaver or something, but it's not-- it wasn't furry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- it was-- kind of looked good on him. It was not--

speaker: interviewer
text: But it wasn't the kind of hat that the Hungarian--

speaker: interviewer
text: So it identified your father a bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- yes, yes. He was a businessman--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and he looked good. He didn't look like a real Hassid. He had a beard, and he had his payots pulled up. And he had--

speaker: interviewer
text: So sort of a modern-- a modern Hassid?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, something like that, with an umbrella, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he wear a black coat? A black frock coat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah but he wore a jacket, which was just not that long. And he would have an overcoat, a black overcoat, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he wear tzitzit? The-- the-- the garb--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but I don't think they showed that.

speaker: interviewee
text: If I remember, that it was always hidden. Because you know, he was on the trains a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe he was just afraid to show it--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother also wore tzitzit?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tzitzit or the fringed garments? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he went on this roller coaster of being a good son and then not wanting religion. So there were problems in my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I know that he went to-- he went to-- because my parents used to travel to Switzerland, because my father sold the Swiss wine. And they took Phillip and they put him into a yeshiva in Montreux, Switzerland, which wasn't Hasidic, but it was religious. And then, they put him even a fancier one in Hungary called Pap, in a town called Pap, P-A-P. And he did very well for a while. But he always left it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, Pap, the yeshiva in Pap, it was a Hasidic yeshiva?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, but it was very famous and very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your goals as a young person, looking towards the future, was to be an actress?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family home sounds wonderful. Your life at home sounds loving.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- my mother read every book. We had Freud's when it came out. We had poetry in the house. We had poetry readings in the afternoon. And my mother had friends who just came to talk to him-- to her about politics. Because she was very, very bright--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you said she was the daughter of a rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Chief rabbi. What town was her father the chief rabbi in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were there for generations in Hajdunanas.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's one you'll need to spell, please?

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's where her father was the chief rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chief rabbi. But for generations they were. And then her brothers became-- and a very well-known family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember what your grandfather-- did he go under a different name like the so-and-so rabbi or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I suppose so, but he was dead when I was born.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my mother always said that his-- that her father was also a beautiful human being. And he couldn't take the First World War, and he died of heart failure.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're coming to the end of our--

speaker: interviewer
text: --tape now. And I-- I'll just ask you one more question, and then we'll end. How did Hitler's rise to power-- in 1933 now, not when he marched into Hungary, but in '33-- affect the life in your Kisvarda?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think they eventually they had the Nuremberg laws, which took away-- we had land also, from my grandparents on my father's side. They took away the land. I couldn't go on to gymnasium.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just high school. And it was-- I think that it was-- everybody was affected by it. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Our tape is up now. And we're going to come back to this--
